title: A Layer-Based Evaluation of Signed Language Writing Systems
subtitle: A comparative demonstration across Filhol, Grushkin, Thiessen, and Unicode SignWriting
summary: A bounded four-case comparison showing how layer discipline clarifies signed language writing, encoding, and standards claims.
abstract: This comparative paper applies the critical review framework to four representative cases: Filhol (2020), Grushkin (2017), Thiessen (2011), and Unicode SignWriting. The comparison does not treat these cases as a complete survey of signed language writing research. Instead, it uses them as a bounded demonstration of a recurring pattern: each work identifies a real part of the signed-writing problem, but the insight is often displaced onto the wrong layer or pressed too far toward becoming the whole answer. The paper argues that signed writing analysis becomes clearer when semantic representation, literacy design, symbol analysis, character standardization, writing systems, text models, and rendering are kept distinct. Its conclusion is methodological as much as evaluative: the field needs layer discipline in order to preserve valid insights without mistaking them for complete solutions.
synopsis: This paper demonstrates the review framework across a bounded core comparison set: Filhol (2020), Grushkin (2017), Thiessen (2011), and Unicode SignWriting. It argues that these works identify real parts of the signed-writing problem but repeatedly displace the writing problem onto another layer, and it includes a comparative matrix as a compact companion.

slides.title: Bounded Comparison
slides.summary: The paper applies the framework to Filhol, Grushkin, Thiessen, and Unicode SignWriting as a compact demonstration set.
slides.speech: This paper is a bounded comparison, not a complete survey. It uses Filhol, Grushkin, Thiessen, and Unicode SignWriting to demonstrate how layer discipline clarifies signed-language writing, encoding, and standards claims.

image_prompts.concrete: Comparative review slide showing four cases: Filhol, Grushkin, Thiessen, and Unicode SignWriting inside a bounded demonstration set
image_prompts.abstract: Four reviewed cases arranged within a clear comparison boundary

slides.title: Recurring Pattern
slides.summary: Each case sees a real part of the problem, but often shifts that insight onto the wrong layer or treats it as the whole answer.
bullets: Grushkin sees the need for writing
Unicode solves part of the character layer
slides.speech: The recurring pattern is that each work sees something real. Filhol sees queryability. Grushkin sees the need for writing. Thiessen sees structure and computation. Unicode solves part of the character layer. The trouble begins when a partial insight is treated as the whole answer.

image_prompts.concrete: Pattern slide showing each reviewed source identifying a real problem layer, then sometimes treating that layer as the whole answer
image_prompts.abstract: Partial insights moving through layers and revealing a recurring review pattern

slides.title: Cross-Case Result
slides.summary: The comparison shows why semantic representation, literacy design, symbol analysis, character standardization, text models, and rendering must remain distinct.
bullets: Text model and rendering are separate layers
slides.speech: The cross-case result is methodological. Signed writing analysis becomes clearer when semantic representation, literacy design, symbol analysis, character standardization, writing systems, text models, and rendering remain distinct.
slides.notes: This slide turns comparison into method.

image_prompts.concrete: Cross-case result slide separating semantic representation, literacy design, symbol analysis, character standardization, text models, and rendering
image_prompts.abstract: Many signed-writing layers laid out as distinct but connected review results

slides.title: Why It Matters
slides.summary: Layer discipline preserves valid insights while preventing incomplete solutions from becoming field-wide conclusions.
slides.speech: The conclusion is that the field needs layer discipline. Without it, useful insights are either dismissed too quickly or overextended into complete solutions. With it, critique can preserve what is valid and build better infrastructure.

image_prompts.concrete: Constructive review slide showing layer discipline preventing false dismissal and overclaiming while preserving useful work
image_prompts.abstract: Layer discipline as a stabilizing bridge between critique and construction
